cards.text: A Labuan MP's appeal against Bersatu's expulsion was dismissed at the Court of Appeal this month. The constitutional question in the case is unanswered.
cards.sub: Article 49A(2)(c), the expulsion carve-out, has been on the books 3 years 7 months. No reported judgment interprets it on its merits.
sources: Radar silence-watch entry, 17 May 2026 — Court of Appeal decision Suhaili Abdul Rahman, 29 April 2026 (specific procedural ground LOW pending CoA grounds-of-judgment)
Federal Constitution Article 49A; Constitution (Amendment) (No. 3) Act 2022 (Act A1664)

cards.h: Article 49A inserted by Constitution (Amendment) (No. 3) Act 2022, in force 5 October 2022.
cards.s: Subsection (2)(c) exempts expulsion from triggering loss of MP status. The carve-out is voter-protective: parties cannot strip an elected MP of their seat by expelling them.
sources: Federal Constitution Article 49A (gazette text, Act A1664, 2022)
Rahmat Lim & Partners, commentary on the Constitution (Amendment) (No. 3) Act 2022
Fulcrum, Malaysia's Anti-hopping Law: Some Loopholes to Mull Over (2022)

cards.h: Zuraida Kamaruddin's case (Court of Appeal December 2024) was decided on contracts law, not Article 49A.
cards.s: The court reduced PKR's RM10 million anti-defection bond to RM100,000, ruling that representatives cannot contract away their constitutional right to choose a party.
sources: Court of Appeal panel led by Datuk See Mee Chun, Zuraida Kamaruddin v PKR, December 2024
Sinar Daily and The Edge Malaysia coverage of the December 2024 ruling

cards.h: Rafizi Ramli and Nik Nazmi (May 2026) used Article 51 to vacate their seats before leaving PKR.
cards.s: Resign seat first, leave party second. The Article 49A trigger never engaged. Anti-hopping protection sidestepped without judicial test.
sources: Transport Minister Anthony Loke statement, 17 May 2026

cards.text: Four years of operative law. Three high-profile cases. Zero substantive judicial interpretation of the carve-out.
sources: Aggregation of the three Article 49A-adjacent matters (Article 49A enactment 2022; Zuraida CoA December 2024; Suhaili CoA April 2026; Rafizi-Nik Nazmi May 2026)

cards.h: A fire alarm installed, never tested
cards.text: The carve-out has been wired into the Constitution since October 2022. No court has been asked to read it on its merits. Its actual scope remains unknown.
sources: Federal Constitution Article 49A; absence of reported judgments interpreting subsection (2)(c)

cards.text: Parties and MPs navigate around the carve-out, not through it. Until a court reads 49A(2)(c) on its merits, what it actually protects remains unsettled.
sources: T4A issue 1993 (voluntary-resignation route); Zuraida 2024 (contracts-law route); Suhaili 2026 (procedural-grounds route)

Federal Constitution Article 49A (Act A1664, 2022); Court of Appeal Zuraida Kamaruddin ruling (December 2024); T4A issue 1993 (Rafizi-Nik Nazmi 17 May 2026); Radar silence-watch entry for Suhaili Abdul Rahman case (29 April 2026 CoA); Rahmat Lim & Partners and Fulcrum commentaries on Article 49A; T4A research brief at engine/briefs/suhaili-bersatu-coa-article-49a.md
confidence: High — primary text of Article 49A is gazetted and verifiable; Zuraida CoA ruling is widely-reported with court records; Rafizi-Nik Nazmi pathway is T4A-encoded as issue 1993. Main remaining uncertainty is the specific procedural ground in the Suhaili 29 April 2026 CoA dismissal (referenced in hook but not used as a basis for substantive claims). The structural argument (four years of law, zero substantive interpretations) holds independently of how exactly the Suhaili case was disposed of.
needs_verification: Specific procedural ground for Court of Appeal dismissal of Suhaili Abdul Rahman appeal, 29 April 2026 (locus standi for party-internal dispute, exhaustion of internal remedies, time-bar, or other) — referenced in hook; Stage 3 to confirm.
Names of the three Court of Appeal judges on the Suhaili panel (29 April 2026) — for completeness if cited in cards.
Election Commission count of seats actually vacated under Article 49A since October 2022 — sharpens the 'untested' framing if the operative count is very low.

Hansard October 2022 second-reading debate — specific MP statements articulating the voter-protective intent of 49A(2)(c). The brief currently asserts this intent; primary-source citation strengthens it.

Whether any first-instance High Court rulings (below the CoA level) have engaged Article 49A(2)(c) on its merits in the 2022-2026 window — the 'zero substantive judgments' claim assumes no such rulings exist.
Bersatu Party Constitution membership-termination clauses (Suhaili background) — affects card 1 (hook) framing but not the substantive cards 2-7.
source_notes: Three of the seven cards stand on primary constitutional or court-record sources (cards 2 fact-Legal, 3 fact-Governance, 4 fact-Political). The hook card cites the radar entry plus the constitutional text — the specific Suhaili facts referenced are flagged in needs_verification. The reframe and view cards aggregate from the three substantive cards. The analogy card is illustrative, not source-dependent. The brief's central argument — that Article 49A(2)(c) has not been judicially interpreted on its merits — is an absence claim. Absence claims are vulnerable to a single counter-finding (a missed first-instance ruling, for instance). Stage 3 should specifically chase whether any High Court or Court of Appeal judgment between October 2022 and April 2026 engaged subsection (2)(c) directly on its merits. If found, the framing must be adjusted from 'untested' to 'rarely tested' or similar.
